name: Sidebar Navigation
description: Sidebar navigation anchors the dashboard experience, presenting sections, account context, and utility links in a persistent frame that feels tailored, editorial, and premium.
summary: Use sidebar navigation for desktop and large-tablet layouts where users need stable movement between overview, reports, clients, invoices, and settings. The active item should be unmistakable without overwhelming adjacent content.

variants.name: expanded
variants.useCase: Primary desktop navigation with labels, icons, and footer account controls.

variants.name: compact
variants.useCase: Tighter desktop layouts that still preserve icon and tooltip support.

variants.name: sectioned
variants.useCase: Information-dense products that group navigation into editorial chapters.

contentGuidelines: Use short nouns for destinations: Overview, Reports, Clients, Library, Settings.
Section headings should group by user mental model rather than backend structure.
Badges should highlight counts or plan context only when the information changes navigation priority.
interactionNotes: Active items should read as a persistent current location, not as a temporary hover state.
Collapsed sidebars require tooltips or an alternate reveal pattern so icons do not become ambiguous.
Account footers can include plan name, workspace identity, or quick switching as long as the region remains visually lighter than primary navigation.

accessibility.semantics: Use nav and list semantics so screen readers can move through the structure efficiently.
accessibility.currentPage: Mark the selected destination with aria-current when it represents the current page.
accessibility.keyboardSupport: All interactive items must be reachable and distinguishable by keyboard alone.

compositionNotes: The sidebar surface should remain visually quieter than primary dashboard cards so data content retains center stage.
Warm accent states help navigation feel tactile and elegant without introducing new color families.
Fraunces is distinctive, so long navigation labels should wrap rarely and only when the information architecture truly demands it.
